claim: Distillation using frontier models as teachers is computationally expensive; using trained student models for validation reduces cost from 1M to minimal requests
actionable: After training student models via distillation, use the student checkpoint for validation instead of the expensive teacher model
source_title: How We Built Zeta2: Training an Edit Prediction Model

claim: Prompt engineering for distillation requires fine-tuning to handle edge cases; models produce inconsistent outputs despite receiving the same input
actionable: When using frontier models for distillation, iterate on prompts carefully and implement repair steps to catch bad predictions
source_title: How We Built Zeta2: Training an Edit Prediction Model

claim: Training examples should sit in middle of Levenshtein distance distribution; too close = obvious, too far = noise
actionable: Filter training examples by distance to settled state; focus on moderate-difficulty examples that show new patterns not in training data
source_title: How We Built Zeta2: Training an Edit Prediction Model

claim: Reversal ratio (how often model undoes user actions) is a key diagnostic metric for catching bad model behavior before shipping
actionable: Track reversal ratio in production evaluation; high ratio indicates model is learning adversarial patterns
source_title: How We Built Zeta2: Training an Edit Prediction Model

claim: Using settled state data for training is inherently noisy due to user mind changes and agent interventions between prediction and settlement
actionable: When using post-hoc labels (settled state), implement filtering to remove training examples where inputs diverged significantly from final outputs
source_title: How We Built Zeta2: Training an Edit Prediction Model
